stories.title: Nothing Ahead — pexels
stories.description: A clip of cats / kittens: Nothing Ahead — pexels
stories.seo_title: Why cats purr — it’s not just happiness 🐱
stories.yt_description: Cats and purring. The most surprising thing about this sound you know so well. Cats don’t just purr when they’re happy—they do it to heal their own bones. Source: Pexels
stories.hook: Cats purr to heal their bones.
stories.script: Cats purr to heal their bones. That’s right—the vibrations actually speed up bone repair. They also purr when they’re stressed, not just happy. Newborns purr while nursing to tell mom they’re okay. So next time you hear that rumble, remember: it’s not just contentment—it’s a built-in recovery tool. What’s your cat’s weirdest habit?
stories.lead: Cats purr to heal their bones. That’s right—the vibrations actually speed up bone repair. They also purr when they’re stressed, not just happy. Newborns purr while nursing to tell mom they’re okay. So next time you hear that rumble, remember: it’s not just contentment—it’s a built-in recovery tool. What’s your cat’s weirdest habit?

stories.title: Havvanur — pexels
stories.description: A clip of cats / kittens: Havvanur — pexels
stories.seo_title: Why cats purr — it’s not just happiness 🐱
stories.yt_description: Cats purr. But did you know they can also use it to heal their own bones? Source: Pexels
stories.hook: Cats purr to fix their bones.
stories.script: Cats purr to fix their bones. That’s right—vets use a cat’s purr to speed up human bone healing. They hum at exactly 25-150 Hz, the same frequency used in bone therapy. And those little chirps when you open a can? They’re actually asking you for food, not just saying hello. So next time your cat curls up on your lap and starts that rumbly purr, they might be fixing themselves up. Why do you think your cat purrs the loudest when you’re sick?
stories.lead: Cats purr to fix their bones. That’s right—vets use a cat’s purr to speed up human bone healing. They hum at exactly 25-150 Hz, the same frequency used in bone therapy. stories.title: celal keser — pexels
stories.description: A clip of cats / kittens: celal keser — pexels
stories.seo_title: Why cats purr — it’s not just happiness 🐱
stories.yt_description: Cats purring. Did you know purring can actually heal broken bones? Source: Pexels
stories.hook: Cats purr to heal their bones.
stories.script: Cats purr to heal their bones. Kittens do it at two days old while they nurse. Adults purr at 25-150 Hz — the frequency used in physical therapy. So next time your cat purrs on your lap, it might be fixing itself. Who else feels like their cat’s a tiny doctor?

stories.title: MC G'Zay — pexels
stories.description: A clip of cats / kittens: MC G'Zay — pexels
stories.seo_title: Cats don’t just purr for happiness — here’s why 🐱
stories.yt_description: Cats purr. But did you know they don’t always do it for happiness. Cats purr at just 25-150 Hz — the same frequency used in healing human bones. Source: Pexels
stories.hook: Cats purr to heal their own bones.
stories.script: Cats purr to heal their own bones. That vibration? 25 to 150 Hz — the exact frequency used in bone healing machines. Kittens purr while nursing and even when they’re in pain. Adults purr when stressed or injured. So next time your cat’s napping, remember: that hum isn’t just happy — it’s medicine.
stories.lead: Cats purr to heal their own bones. That vibration? 25 to 150 Hz — the exact frequency used in bone healing machines. Kittens purr while nursing and even when they’re in pain. Adults purr when stressed or injured. So next time your cat’s napping, remember: that hum isn’t just happy — it’s medicine.

stories.title: Anna Bondarenko — pexels
stories.description: A clip of dogs / puppies: Anna Bondarenko — pexels
stories.seo_title: Dogs see in colour — but not the way you think 🐶
stories.yt_description: Dogs see in colour — but not the way you think. Their colour range is like a red-green colour-blind human. Source: Pexels
stories.script: Dogs see in colour. Not like us, though — their world’s more muted, like a red-green colour-blind person. Blues and yellows pop for them; reds and greens? Mostly grey. Their night vision? Four times better than ours thanks to a special mirror-like layer in their eyes. Here’s the weird bit: they can spot a blue toy against green grass, but a red one just blends in. Next time you pick a toy, think about their eyesight.

stories.title: bushmaster Roll — pexels
stories.description: A clip of dogs / puppies: bushmaster Roll — pexels
stories.seo_title: Dogs remember faces not smells — here’s how
stories.yt_description: Dogs remember faces not smells. The latest study shows pups recall human faces like we do. Source: Pexels
stories.script: Dogs remember faces not smells. It’s like they’ve got their own built-in facial recognition. They can match a face to a treat in just a few glances. Even after years apart, a dog will still recognize you. Who else remembers faces that well? Drop your pick in the comments.

stories.title: JUN HO LEE — pexels
stories.description: A clip of marine life in the ocean: JUN HO LEE — pexels
stories.seo_title: Why jellyfish don't have brains — and how they thrive
stories.yt_description: Why jellyfish don't have brains — and how they thrive. Jellyfish have existed for over 500 million years, outliving dinosaurs. Source: Pexels
stories.script: Jellyfish don’t need brains. They’ve been around for 500 million years, outlasting dinosaurs. Their bodies are 95% water and they sting without a nervous system. They just drift, eat, and reproduce. So… why do we still fear them?
stories.lead: Jellyfish don’t need brains. They’ve been around for 500 million years, outlasting dinosaurs. Their bodies are 95% water and they sting without a nervous system. They just drift, eat, and reproduce. So… why do we still fear them?

stories.title: Juliana Finamore — pexels
stories.description: A clip of marine life in the ocean: Juliana Finamore — pexels
stories.seo_title: Reason octopuses have three hearts 🐙
stories.yt_description: Octopuses have three hearts—two pump blood to their gills and one to their body. When they swim, the heart that supplies their body actually stops beating. Source: Pexels
stories.script: Octopuses have three hearts. Two pump blood to their gills, one to their body. When they swim fast, that third heart actually stops beating. So if you see one jet away, it’s literally holding its breath. Ever watched an octopus chill on the reef? Those are the moments they’re giving their hearts a break. What’s the wildest heart fact you’ve heard?

stories.title: Gabriele Brancati — pexels
stories.description: A clip of wild animals in nature: Gabriele Brancati — pexels
stories.seo_title: Wild animals do this to stay alive nobody tells you
stories.yt_description: Wild animals use clever tricks to survive. Some play dead so perfectly predators lose interest. Source: Pexels
stories.hook: Some wild animals fake their own deaths
stories.script: Some wild animals fake their own deaths. Opossums go completely limp and even secrete a corpse-like smell. A snake called the hognose does the same thing, flipping upside down with its tongue out. Moths play dead mid-air just by folding their wings. Bet you can’t tell which one’s faking in this clip.
stories.lead: Some wild animals fake their own deaths. Opossums go completely limp and even secrete a corpse-like smell. A snake called the hognose does the same thing, flipping upside down with its tongue out. Moths play dead mid-air just by folding their wings. Bet you can’t tell which one’s faking in this clip.

stories.title: Simone Cappellari — pexels
stories.description: A clip of wild animals in nature: Simone Cappellari — pexels
stories.seo_title: Why wild animals huddle together — they share food
stories.yt_description: Why wild animals huddle together. The most surprising reason isn’t warmth—it’s shared food. Source: Pexels
stories.script: They huddle to share food. Meerkats rotate sentinel duty so everyone eats. Wild dogs regurgitate meals for pups. Even elephants will pass mouthfuls to their herd. Next time you see a pack, they’re literally splitting the bill. Who do you think is the best food sharer in the wild?
stories.lead: They huddle to share food. Meerkats rotate sentinel duty so everyone eats. Wild dogs regurgitate meals for pups. Even elephants will pass mouthfuls to their herd. Next time you see a pack, they’re literally splitting the bill. Who do you think is the best food sharer in the wild?

stories.title: TBD Tuyên — pexels
stories.description: A clip of wild animals in nature: TBD Tuyên — pexels
stories.seo_title: Why these wild animals are actually geniuses 🐺
stories.yt_description: These wild animals aren’t just surviving—they’re solving problems. Here’s the part nobody tells you: some species use tools, plan ahead, and even teach each other. Source: Pexels
stories.hook: They plan ahead like chess players.
stories.script: They plan ahead like chess players. Hyenas crack bones with rocks like we’d use a hammer. Ravens drop nuts on roads so cars crack them open. Even squirrels bury fake nuts to trick competitors. Who’s the real genius here? What’s an animal you’d bet is clever?
stories.lead: They plan ahead like chess players. Hyenas crack bones with rocks like we’d use a hammer. Ravens drop nuts on roads so cars crack them open. Even squirrels bury fake nuts to trick competitors. Who’s the real genius here? What’s an animal you’d bet is clever?

stories.title: ROMAN ODINTSOV — pexels
stories.description: A clip of birds: ROMAN ODINTSOV — pexels
stories.seo_title: Why birds bob their heads — it is not what you think
stories.yt_description: Birds bob their heads when they walk. It’s not about balance—it’s about vision. Source: Pexels
stories.script: Birds don’t walk—they snap. That head bob isn’t for balance, it’s for vision. When they move, their eyes lock on prey while their body catches up. Think of it like a tiny, feathered camera stabilizer. Next time you see a pigeon strut, remember: it’s not swagger, it’s science. What’s the weirdest bird motion you’ve seen? Let me know in the comments.

stories.title: Adili Family — pexels
stories.description: A clip of farm animals: Adili Family — pexels
stories.seo_title: Why cows have best friends and get stressed when separated 🐄
stories.yt_description: Cows are social animals with best friends. They get stressed when separated from their chosen buddy. Source: Pexels
stories.script: Cows have best friends. They pick one cow to bond with, just like we do. When separated, their stress levels spike—heart rate goes up, they call out more. Best part? You can see it in their eyes and ears. Ever notice a cow looking 'sad'? That might be why. Do you think animals choose friends like we do?
stories.lead: Cows have best friends. They pick one cow to bond with, just like we do. When separated, their stress levels spike—heart rate goes up, they call out more. Best part? You can see it in their eyes and ears. Ever notice a cow looking 'sad'? That might be why. Do you think animals choose friends like we do?

stories.title: Ali Akdemir — pexels
stories.description: A clip of farm animals: Ali Akdemir — pexels
stories.seo_title: Why farm animals have stomachs you won't believe 🐑
stories.yt_description: Farm animals have a stomach design that evolved over 50 million years. A cow's stomach can hold up to 50 gallons of food and liquid. Source: Pexels
stories.script: Cows have four stomachs. They’re not separate organs, but one stomach with four compartments. The first one, the rumen, ferments food before it’s chewed again—yep, they burp up their lunch to chew it a second time. Chickens have something even weirder: they swallow rocks to grind their food. Ever seen a chicken pecking at gravel? Now you know why. So, which farm animal’s digestion blew your mind more?

stories.title: Marina Leonova — pexels
stories.description: A clip of farm animals: Marina Leonova — pexels
stories.seo_title: Why pigs love mud — it’s not just for fun 🐷
stories.yt_description: Pigs love mud. Here's why they wallow. Pigs can't sweat, so mud keeps them cool. Source: Pexels
stories.script: Pigs can’t sweat. So they wallow in mud like it’s a spa day. The mud cools them down and keeps sunburn away. It also hides their scent from predators. What’s your go-to summer cool-down trick?
stories.lead: Pigs can’t sweat. So they wallow in mud like it’s a spa day. The mud cools them down and keeps sunburn away. It also hides their scent from predators. What’s your go-to summer cool-down trick?
